selection_rule: Specific condition/device item IDs plus targeted exact disability/accommodation listings; broad unrelated category entries were excluded.

condition index.category_id: Index all extracted condition/domain/device/listing profiles from both input JSON files and show which category files contain them.

items.item_name: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder when untreated or unsupported

brain nervous system cognitive.title: How disabilities and disorders affect the brain, nervous system, cognition, attention, memory, perception, communication, and executive functioning

items.item_name: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder when untreated or unsupported
source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for: Untreated ADHD can cause problems throughout life, including school, work, relationships, and other aspects of life.

Children with untreated ADHD may struggle with paying attention in class, learning taught material, falling behind, poor grades, emotion control, social problems, making or keeping friends, low self-esteem, depression, impulsive injuries, and more emergency room visits for injuries.

Teenagers with untreated ADHD may continue poor school performance, struggle with relationships, conflict with parents, engage in drinking, smoking, drug use, risky sexual choices, have higher rates of eating disorders among girls, and be at higher risk for car accidents.

Adults with untreated ADHD may have employment problems, difficulty arriving on time, completing deadlines, staying organized, getting along with coworkers, accepting criticism calmly, relationship problems, higher divorce rates, depression, low self-esteem, drinking, smoking, drug abuse, risky sex, car accidents, gambling problems, trouble with the law, and elevated representation in prison populations.

mental_cognitive: Short attention span, executive dysfunction, poor organization, difficulty completing deadlines, difficulty tracking instructions, time blindness, working-memory strain, and inconsistent task follow-through.
A person may understand what needs to be done but still be unable to initiate, sequence, prioritize, or sustain the task without supports.

raw_original_entry_pointer.note: Full original entry and raw source text are preserved in the original_source_jsons folder and source preservation map.

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for: ASD is a developmental disability caused by differences in the brain; some people have a known difference such as a genetic condition, while other causes are not yet known.
Scientists believe multiple causes act together to change common developmental pathways, and much remains to learn about causes and impacts.
People with ASD may behave, communicate, interact, and learn differently; appearance often does not set them apart.
Abilities vary significantly: some autistic people have advanced conversation skills, others are nonverbal; some need a lot of daily help, others work and live with little to no support.

ASD begins before age 3 and can last throughout life, though symptoms may change over time. Some children show symptoms in the first 12 months; others later; some lose skills between 18 and 24 months.
Adolescents and young adults may have difficulty developing and maintaining friendships, communicating with peers/adults, and understanding expected school or work behavior.
Anxiety, depression, and ADHD occur more often in people with ASD than in people without ASD.

Signs include problems with social communication/interaction, restricted or repetitive behaviors or interests, and different ways of learning, moving, or paying attention. These characteristics can make life very challenging.
Risk factors can include having a sibling with ASD, certain genetic/chromosomal conditions such as fragile X or tuberous sclerosis, birth complications, and older parents.

Diagnosis can be difficult because there is no blood test; clinicians look at behavior and development. ASD can sometimes be detected at 18 months or younger, and diagnosis by an experienced professional by age 2 can be reliable, but many are diagnosed much later or in adolescence/adulthood.

Delayed diagnosis means people might not get early help. Current treatments seek to reduce symptoms that interfere with daily functioning and quality of life, and plans are individualized and often involve multiple professionals.
Early intervention services can greatly improve a child’s development.

mental_cognitive: Different communication, learning, attention, and behavior patterns can affect school, work, healthcare, relationships, and daily living.
Some people require extensive support for daily life while others require minimal support; both can still need accommodations.
support_variability: The spectrum includes people with advanced conversation skills and nonverbal people, people needing major daily support, and people living or working independently. The JSON should not assume one autistic presentation.

raw_original_entry_pointer.note: Full original entry and raw source text are preserved in the original_source_jsons folder and source preservation map.

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for: BPD affects how people feel about themselves and others, making it hard to function in everyday life.
BPD includes unstable intense relationships, impulsiveness, unhealthy self-image, strong fear of abandonment, mood swings, anger, impulsiveness, and self-injury that may push others away.

Symptoms may include fear of abandonment, unstable intense relationships, quick changes in self-image, stress-related paranoia or loss of contact with reality, risky behavior, suicide threats or self-injury, mood swings, emptiness, and inappropriate strong anger.
Causes are not fully known, but environmental factors such as childhood abuse or neglect, genetics, and brain changes may be involved.

Risk factors include hereditary predisposition and stressful childhood experiences such as abuse, neglect, separation, caregiver substance misuse or mental health issues, hostile conflict, and unstable family relationships.

Complications may include job changes or losses, not finishing education, legal issues, conflict-filled relationships, marital stress or divorce, self-injury, hospital stays, abusive relationships, unplanned pregnancies, STIs, motor vehicle accidents, physical fights, attempted suicide or death by suicide, and other mental health conditions including depression, substance misuse, anxiety, eating disorders, bipolar disorder, PTSD, ADHD, and other personality disorders.

mental_cognitive: Stress-related paranoia or loss of contact with reality can last minutes to hours.
Rapid shifts in goals, values, and self-image can disrupt planning, continuity, and decision-making.

raw_original_entry_pointer.note: Full original entry and raw source text are preserved in the original_source_jsons folder and source preservation map.

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for: MS causes breakdown of the protective covering of nerves and may cause numbness, weakness, trouble walking, vision changes, and other symptoms.
In MS, the immune system attacks the myelin sheath covering nerve fibers, interrupting communication between the brain and body and potentially causing permanent nerve damage.
Symptoms depend on the person, nervous-system damage location, and damage severity. Some people lose the ability to walk independently or move at all; others have long remission periods.
Types include relapsing-remitting MS, secondary-progressive MS, primary-progressive MS, clinically isolated syndrome, and radiologically isolated syndrome.

Common symptoms include numbness or tingling, electric shock sensations with neck movement, lack of coordination, trouble walking or inability to walk, weakness, partial or complete vision loss, double vision, blurry vision, vertigo, sexual/bowel/bladder dysfunction, fatigue, slurred speech, memory/thinking/understanding trouble, and mood changes.
Small body-temperature increases can temporarily worsen symptoms as pseudorelapses.
Risk factors include age, sex, family history, Epstein-Barr and other infections, race, climate, vitamin D, obesity, autoimmune disease, smoking, and gut microbiome differences.

Complications may include mood changes such as depression/anxiety/mood swings, muscle stiffness/spasms, rare seizures, severe weakness or paralysis, bladder/bowel/sexual dysfunction, and thinking/memory trouble.

mental_cognitive: MS can cause trouble with memory, thinking, and understanding information.
Cognitive fatigue may make complex forms, appointments, transportation planning, employment, school, and social communication harder.

raw_original_entry_pointer.note: Full original entry and raw source text are preserved in the original_source_jsons folder and source preservation map.

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for: PMOS is the new name for the condition previously known as PCOS, affecting 1 in 8 or more than 170 million women worldwide.
PMOS is characterized by fluctuations in hormones with impacts on weight, metabolic and mental health, skin, and the reproductive system.
The previous name reduced a complex long-term hormonal/endocrine disorder to a misunderstanding about cysts and focus on ovaries, contributing to missed diagnoses and inadequate treatment.
Experts reported that there is no increase in abnormal cysts on the ovary in the condition, and diverse features were often unappreciated.

The name-change process involved more than 50 patient and professional organizations, 56 organizations in the article body, more than 22,000 survey responses, multiple workshops, lived-experience experts, and a 14-year collaboration.
The three-year transition period is supported by an international education and awareness campaign, with full implementation planned in the 2028 International Guideline update.
Name-change principles included patient benefit, scientific accuracy, ease of communication, avoidance of stigma, cultural appropriateness, and accompanying implementation.

mental_cognitive: The source specifically identifies mental health impact as part of the condition’s broader profile.
Delayed diagnosis and inadequate care can create long-term confusion, self-blame, and difficulty understanding the body’s endocrine/metabolic needs.

raw_original_entry_pointer.note: Full original entry and raw source text are preserved in the original_source_jsons folder and source preservation map.

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for: Reactive attachment disorder in adults often occurs when RAD was not treated during childhood.
RAD is described as a serious disorder where a child is unable to form strong attachments with a primary caregiver.

Adult signs can include detachment, withdrawal, difficulty keeping relationships, lack of affectionate expression, control issues, anger outbursts, impulsiveness, distrustfulness, inability to fully grasp emotions, feelings of emptiness, and lack of belonging.
RAD can drastically impact meaningful connections and may result in mental health problems and drug abuse.
RAD is not autism; RAD is associated with neglect and abuse rather than the same roots as autism spectrum disorder.
Treatment is individualized and may include CBT and DBT; co-occurring substance use disorder should be addressed simultaneously.

mental_cognitive: Difficulty interpreting safety, trust, affection, rejection, and relational intent can affect decision-making and communication.
Control patterns and distrust can make collaborative planning hard without trauma-informed support.

raw_original_entry_pointer.note: Full original entry and raw source text are preserved in the original_source_jsons folder and source preservation map.

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for: Underactive thyroid gland cannot make enough thyroid hormone to keep the body running normally; common causes include autoimmune disease, surgical thyroid removal, and radiation treatment.

category_impacts.mental_cognitive: Endocrine instability can require symptom tracking, medication timing, lab monitoring, and crisis planning; fatigue, sleep problems, anxiety, and metabolic symptoms can affect concentration and decision-making.

category_impacts.mental_cognitive: Flares, pain, medication schedules, medical appointments, and uncertainty can create constant planning and decision fatigue.

source_page_references.why_used: mobility aid definitions, device types, selection considerations, advantages, limitations, and usage guidance

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for.source_uses: Manual wheelchair is for locomotion indoors and outdoors for people with mobility disability, injury, or surgery recovery; wheelchairs can enable mobility, independence, and everyday activities despite reduced mobility.

limitations_and_risks: Self-propulsion can require shoulder, arm, hand, trunk, and stamina capacity; transport chairs can make the user dependent on another person for propulsion.
A building can be technically close by but unusable because of steps, steep ramps, heavy doors, narrow turns, inaccessible bathrooms, cluttered aisles, gravel, snow, or curb cuts that do not line up.
Standard taxis, rideshares, and private cars may not fit the chair or may require unsafe transfers; if the wheelchair does not fit, the person may be stranded.
If a wheelchair user can transfer but the chair is left behind, the person may lose independence, safe seating, pressure relief, posture support, or the ability to leave.

category_impacts.mental_cognitive: Choosing and using an aid adds planning: fitting, maintenance, repair, charging, transport compatibility, route access, battery range, backups, and insurance/billing.

raw_original_entry_pointer.note: Full original entry and raw source text are preserved in the original_source_jsons folder and source preservation map.

source_page_references.why_used: mobility aid definitions, device types, selection considerations, advantages, limitations, and usage guidance

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for.source_uses: Used by individuals with medical conditions and difficulty walking who cannot use a manual wheelchair or electric scooter; usable indoors and outdoors; can include tight-turn home models, heavy-duty outdoor models, power seating such as recline/tilt/stand, and customizations.

limitations_and_risks: Power chairs give independence only while the environment and infrastructure cooperate: accessible entrances, working elevators, charged batteries, safe curb cuts, ramp grade, door widths, turning radius, accessible transportation, and repair access all matter.

If the battery dies, joystick/controller fails, tire fails, or charger is unavailable, the chair can become an immobilized piece of heavy equipment. If the user can walk a little, they may have to get out and push or seek help; if they cannot walk or cannot safely transfer, they can be stuck in place.
Power chairs are difficult or impossible for many people to manually push due to weight, drive locks, slopes, terrain, and caregiver strain.
A standard cab or rideshare may not take the chair. A wheelchair-accessible vehicle may be unavailable, late, too expensive, or unable to fit a specific large power chair.

Emergency care can separate a person from the chair. Standard ambulances may require transfer to a stretcher and may lack a lift, ramp, tie-down, or storage for a large customized chair; leaving the chair behind can mean the person arrives at a hospital without mobility, seating, pressure relief, or a safe way home.

category_impacts.mental_cognitive: Choosing and using an aid adds planning: fitting, maintenance, repair, charging, transport compatibility, route access, battery range, backups, and insurance/billing.

raw_original_entry_pointer.note: Full original entry and raw source text are preserved in the original_source_jsons folder and source preservation map.

psychological emotional.title: Psychological and emotional impacts: fear, shame, grief, anxiety, identity, self-worth, mood, trauma, regulation, and distress

items.item_name: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder when untreated or unsupported
source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for: Untreated ADHD can cause problems throughout life, including school, work, relationships, and other aspects of life.

Children with untreated ADHD may struggle with paying attention in class, learning taught material, falling behind, poor grades, emotion control, social problems, making or keeping friends, low self-esteem, depression, impulsive injuries, and more emergency room visits for injuries.

Teenagers with untreated ADHD may continue poor school performance, struggle with relationships, conflict with parents, engage in drinking, smoking, drug use, risky sexual choices, have higher rates of eating disorders among girls, and be at higher risk for car accidents.

Adults with untreated ADHD may have employment problems, difficulty arriving on time, completing deadlines, staying organized, getting along with coworkers, accepting criticism calmly, relationship problems, higher divorce rates, depression, low self-esteem, drinking, smoking, drug abuse, risky sex, car accidents, gambling problems, trouble with the law, and elevated representation in prison populations.

psychological: Repeated criticism and failure without treatment can create shame, low self-esteem, depression, anxiety, and a belief that the person is broken or lazy rather than unsupported.
Untreated ADHD can make a person vulnerable to punishment-based systems that treat symptoms as character flaws.
emotional: Difficulty controlling emotions can affect school, family, friendships, work, and romantic relationships.
Emotional impulsivity may cause arguments, rejection sensitivity, regret, and intense distress after mistakes.

raw_original_entry_pointer.note: Full original entry and raw source text are preserved in the original_source_jsons folder and source preservation map.

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for: ASD is a developmental disability caused by differences in the brain; some people have a known difference such as a genetic condition, while other causes are not yet known.
Scientists believe multiple causes act together to change common developmental pathways, and much remains to learn about causes and impacts.
People with ASD may behave, communicate, interact, and learn differently; appearance often does not set them apart.
Abilities vary significantly: some autistic people have advanced conversation skills, others are nonverbal; some need a lot of daily help, others work and live with little to no support.

ASD begins before age 3 and can last throughout life, though symptoms may change over time. Some children show symptoms in the first 12 months; others later; some lose skills between 18 and 24 months.
Adolescents and young adults may have difficulty developing and maintaining friendships, communicating with peers/adults, and understanding expected school or work behavior.
Anxiety, depression, and ADHD occur more often in people with ASD than in people without ASD.

Risk factors can include having a sibling with ASD, certain genetic/chromosomal conditions such as fragile X or tuberous sclerosis, birth complications, and older parents.

Diagnosis can be difficult because there is no blood test; clinicians look at behavior and development. ASD can sometimes be detected at 18 months or younger, and diagnosis by an experienced professional by age 2 can be reliable, but many are diagnosed much later or in adolescence/adulthood.

Delayed diagnosis means people might not get early help. Current treatments seek to reduce symptoms that interfere with daily functioning and quality of life, and plans are individualized and often involve multiple professionals.
Early intervention services can greatly improve a child’s development.

psychological: Late diagnosis or lack of recognition can cause years of being misunderstood, punished, or treated as intentionally difficult.
Co-occurring anxiety, depression, and ADHD are more common and may be worsened by unsupported environments.
emotional: Misunderstood communication and sensory overload can produce fear, shame, frustration, loneliness, and exhaustion.
A person may internalize repeated social correction as personal failure if autism is not recognized and accommodated.

raw_original_entry_pointer.note: Full original entry and raw source text are preserved in the original_source_jsons folder and source preservation map.

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for: BPD affects how people feel about themselves and others, making it hard to function in everyday life.
BPD includes unstable intense relationships, impulsiveness, unhealthy self-image, strong fear of abandonment, mood swings, anger, impulsiveness, and self-injury that may push others away.

Symptoms may include fear of abandonment, unstable intense relationships, quick changes in self-image, stress-related paranoia or loss of contact with reality, risky behavior, suicide threats or self-injury, mood swings, emptiness, and inappropriate strong anger.
Causes are not fully known, but environmental factors such as childhood abuse or neglect, genetics, and brain changes may be involved.

Risk factors include hereditary predisposition and stressful childhood experiences such as abuse, neglect, separation, caregiver substance misuse or mental health issues, hostile conflict, and unstable family relationships.

Complications may include job changes or losses, not finishing education, legal issues, conflict-filled relationships, marital stress or divorce, self-injury, hospital stays, abusive relationships, unplanned pregnancies, STIs, motor vehicle accidents, physical fights, attempted suicide or death by suicide, and other mental health conditions including depression, substance misuse, anxiety, eating disorders, bipolar disorder, PTSD, ADHD, and other personality disorders.

psychological: Fear of abandonment and unstable identity can make the person feel unsafe in ordinary relationship stress.
Without treatment, shame, crisis cycles, self-blame, rejection sensitivity, and trauma responses can intensify.
emotional: Wide mood swings, intense anger, anxiety, shame, emptiness, and extreme abandonment fear can be exhausting and frightening.
The emotional pain can be severe enough to contribute to self-harm or suicidal thoughts.

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for: MS causes breakdown of the protective covering of nerves and may cause numbness, weakness, trouble walking, vision changes, and other symptoms.
In MS, the immune system attacks the myelin sheath covering nerve fibers, interrupting communication between the brain and body and potentially causing permanent nerve damage.
Symptoms depend on the person, nervous-system damage location, and damage severity. Some people lose the ability to walk independently or move at all; others have long remission periods.
Types include relapsing-remitting MS, secondary-progressive MS, primary-progressive MS, clinically isolated syndrome, and radiologically isolated syndrome.

Common symptoms include numbness or tingling, electric shock sensations with neck movement, lack of coordination, trouble walking or inability to walk, weakness, partial or complete vision loss, double vision, blurry vision, vertigo, sexual/bowel/bladder dysfunction, fatigue, slurred speech, memory/thinking/understanding trouble, and mood changes.
Small body-temperature increases can temporarily worsen symptoms as pseudorelapses.
Risk factors include age, sex, family history, Epstein-Barr and other infections, race, climate, vitamin D, obesity, autoimmune disease, smoking, and gut microbiome differences.

Complications may include mood changes such as depression/anxiety/mood swings, muscle stiffness/spasms, rare seizures, severe weakness or paralysis, bladder/bowel/sexual dysfunction, and thinking/memory trouble.

psychological: Unpredictable relapses, permanent nerve damage risk, mobility loss, vision change, and loss of independence can cause fear, grief, trauma, anxiety, and depression.
A person may have to constantly calculate whether an environment is safe, accessible, temperature-controlled, and close enough to bathrooms or exits.
emotional: Fatigue and physical decline can create frustration, shame, grief, vulnerability, and fear of being stranded or dependent on unreliable systems.

raw_original_entry_pointer.note: Full original entry and raw source text are preserved in the original_source_jsons folder and source preservation map.

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for: PMOS is the new name for the condition previously known as PCOS, affecting 1 in 8 or more than 170 million women worldwide.
PMOS is characterized by fluctuations in hormones with impacts on weight, metabolic and mental health, skin, and the reproductive system.
The previous name reduced a complex long-term hormonal/endocrine disorder to a misunderstanding about cysts and focus on ovaries, contributing to missed diagnoses and inadequate treatment.
Experts reported that there is no increase in abnormal cysts on the ovary in the condition, and diverse features were often unappreciated.

The name-change process involved more than 50 patient and professional organizations, 56 organizations in the article body, more than 22,000 survey responses, multiple workshops, lived-experience experts, and a 14-year collaboration.
The three-year transition period is supported by an international education and awareness campaign, with full implementation planned in the 2028 International Guideline update.
Name-change principles included patient benefit, scientific accuracy, ease of communication, avoidance of stigma, cultural appropriateness, and accompanying implementation.

psychological: Stigma and misunderstanding can be intensified when the condition is framed incorrectly as only about cysts or reproductive organs.
Medical dismissal can damage trust in healthcare and discourage future care-seeking.
emotional: Delayed diagnosis, inadequate care, symptoms affecting skin/weight/reproduction, and culturally stigmatizing language can create distress, shame, frustration, grief, and anger.
A scientifically accurate and less stigmatizing name can support recognition and validation.

raw_original_entry_pointer.note: Full original entry and raw source text are preserved in the original_source_jsons folder and source preservation map.

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for: Reactive attachment disorder in adults often occurs when RAD was not treated during childhood.
RAD is described as a serious disorder where a child is unable to form strong attachments with a primary caregiver.

Adult signs can include detachment, withdrawal, difficulty keeping relationships, lack of affectionate expression, control issues, anger outbursts, impulsiveness, distrustfulness, inability to fully grasp emotions, feelings of emptiness, and lack of belonging.
RAD can drastically impact meaningful connections and may result in mental health problems and drug abuse.
RAD is not autism; RAD is associated with neglect and abuse rather than the same roots as autism spectrum disorder.
Treatment is individualized and may include CBT and DBT; co-occurring substance use disorder should be addressed simultaneously.

psychological: RAD can compromise positive sense of self and overall mental health.
The disorder is linked to early neglect, abuse, caregiver disruption, foster care movement, orphanage care, traumatic losses, or loss of a caretaker after bonding.
emotional: Emptiness, lack of belonging, difficulty receiving love, anger, impulsivity, and resistance to affection can be central emotional burdens.
Support can feel unsafe, intrusive, conditional, or threatening if the person learned early that attachment was unreliable.

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for.source_uses: Manual wheelchair is for locomotion indoors and outdoors for people with mobility disability, injury, or surgery recovery; wheelchairs can enable mobility, independence, and everyday activities despite reduced mobility.

limitations_and_risks: Self-propulsion can require shoulder, arm, hand, trunk, and stamina capacity; transport chairs can make the user dependent on another person for propulsion.
A building can be technically close by but unusable because of steps, steep ramps, heavy doors, narrow turns, inaccessible bathrooms, cluttered aisles, gravel, snow, or curb cuts that do not line up.
Standard taxis, rideshares, and private cars may not fit the chair or may require unsafe transfers; if the wheelchair does not fit, the person may be stranded.
If a wheelchair user can transfer but the chair is left behind, the person may lose independence, safe seating, pressure relief, posture support, or the ability to leave.

category_impacts.psychological: A good aid can restore confidence; inaccessible reactions and public scrutiny can also create anxiety, hypervigilance, and fear of being trapped or abandoned.
category_impacts.emotional: Aids can bring relief and independence, but also grief, frustration, embarrassment from stigma, and anger when access failures blame the disabled person instead of the environment.

source_page_references.why_used: mobility aid definitions, device types, selection considerations, advantages, limitations, and usage guidance

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for.source_uses: Used by individuals with medical conditions and difficulty walking who cannot use a manual wheelchair or electric scooter; usable indoors and outdoors; can include tight-turn home models, heavy-duty outdoor models, power seating such as recline/tilt/stand, and customizations.

limitations_and_risks: Power chairs give independence only while the environment and infrastructure cooperate: accessible entrances, working elevators, charged batteries, safe curb cuts, ramp grade, door widths, turning radius, accessible transportation, and repair access all matter.

If the battery dies, joystick/controller fails, tire fails, or charger is unavailable, the chair can become an immobilized piece of heavy equipment. If the user can walk a little, they may have to get out and push or seek help; if they cannot walk or cannot safely transfer, they can be stuck in place.
Power chairs are difficult or impossible for many people to manually push due to weight, drive locks, slopes, terrain, and caregiver strain.
A standard cab or rideshare may not take the chair. A wheelchair-accessible vehicle may be unavailable, late, too expensive, or unable to fit a specific large power chair.

Emergency care can separate a person from the chair. Standard ambulances may require transfer to a stretcher and may lack a lift, ramp, tie-down, or storage for a large customized chair; leaving the chair behind can mean the person arrives at a hospital without mobility, seating, pressure relief, or a safe way home.

category_impacts.psychological: A good aid can restore confidence; inaccessible reactions and public scrutiny can also create anxiety, hypervigilance, and fear of being trapped or abandoned.
category_impacts.emotional: Aids can bring relief and independence, but also grief, frustration, embarrassment from stigma, and anger when access failures blame the disabled person instead of the environment.

accommodations absent.title: Needed accommodations and the effects when accommodations are absent, denied, inconsistent, or treated as afterthoughts

items.item_name: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder when untreated or unsupported
source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for: Untreated ADHD can cause problems throughout life, including school, work, relationships, and other aspects of life.

Children with untreated ADHD may struggle with paying attention in class, learning taught material, falling behind, poor grades, emotion control, social problems, making or keeping friends, low self-esteem, depression, impulsive injuries, and more emergency room visits for injuries.

Teenagers with untreated ADHD may continue poor school performance, struggle with relationships, conflict with parents, engage in drinking, smoking, drug use, risky sexual choices, have higher rates of eating disorders among girls, and be at higher risk for car accidents.

Adults with untreated ADHD may have employment problems, difficulty arriving on time, completing deadlines, staying organized, getting along with coworkers, accepting criticism calmly, relationship problems, higher divorce rates, depression, low self-esteem, drinking, smoking, drug abuse, risky sex, car accidents, gambling problems, trouble with the law, and elevated representation in prison populations.

helpful: Medication management where appropriate, therapy/coaching, school or workplace accommodations, written instructions, reminders, structured routines, deadline scaffolding, reduced-distraction spaces, flexible scheduling, executive-function tools, and emotional-regulation supports.
effect_if_absent: The person may be punished for symptoms, lose educational or job opportunities, experience social rejection, develop low self-esteem or depression, and become more vulnerable to risky behavior or legal consequences.
Without accommodations, ADHD-related difficulty can be misread as unwillingness rather than a need for structure and support.

The person may be punished for symptoms, lose educational or job opportunities, experience social rejection, develop low self-esteem or depression, and become more vulnerable to risky behavior or legal consequences.
Without accommodations, ADHD-related difficulty can be misread as unwillingness rather than a need for structure and support.

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for: ASD is a developmental disability caused by differences in the brain; some people have a known difference such as a genetic condition, while other causes are not yet known.
Scientists believe multiple causes act together to change common developmental pathways, and much remains to learn about causes and impacts.
People with ASD may behave, communicate, interact, and learn differently; appearance often does not set them apart.
Abilities vary significantly: some autistic people have advanced conversation skills, others are nonverbal; some need a lot of daily help, others work and live with little to no support.

ASD begins before age 3 and can last throughout life, though symptoms may change over time. Some children show symptoms in the first 12 months; others later; some lose skills between 18 and 24 months.
Adolescents and young adults may have difficulty developing and maintaining friendships, communicating with peers/adults, and understanding expected school or work behavior.
Anxiety, depression, and ADHD occur more often in people with ASD than in people without ASD.

Signs include problems with social communication/interaction, restricted or repetitive behaviors or interests, and different ways of learning, moving, or paying attention. These characteristics can make life very challenging.
Risk factors can include having a sibling with ASD, certain genetic/chromosomal conditions such as fragile X or tuberous sclerosis, birth complications, and older parents.

Diagnosis can be difficult because there is no blood test; clinicians look at behavior and development. ASD can sometimes be detected at 18 months or younger, and diagnosis by an experienced professional by age 2 can be reliable, but many are diagnosed much later or in adolescence/adulthood.

Delayed diagnosis means people might not get early help. Current treatments seek to reduce symptoms that interfere with daily functioning and quality of life, and plans are individualized and often involve multiple professionals.
Early intervention services can greatly improve a child’s development.

helpful: Early intervention when relevant, individualized treatment plans, developmental services, speech/communication supports, occupational therapy when needed, sensory accommodations, quiet spaces, clear written expectations, predictable schedules, alternative communication, flexible social demands, remote options, and support for anxiety/depression/ADHD.
effect_if_absent: Without accommodations, autistic traits may be punished as rudeness, defiance, laziness, immaturity, or lack of effort.
Unsupported sensory and social environments can cause shutdown, meltdown, withdrawal, school refusal, job loss, depression, anxiety, and loss of trust.
Without accommodations, autistic traits may be punished as rudeness, defiance, laziness, immaturity, or lack of effort.
Unsupported sensory and social environments can cause shutdown, meltdown, withdrawal, school refusal, job loss, depression, anxiety, and loss of trust.

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for: BPD affects how people feel about themselves and others, making it hard to function in everyday life.
BPD includes unstable intense relationships, impulsiveness, unhealthy self-image, strong fear of abandonment, mood swings, anger, impulsiveness, and self-injury that may push others away.

Symptoms may include fear of abandonment, unstable intense relationships, quick changes in self-image, stress-related paranoia or loss of contact with reality, risky behavior, suicide threats or self-injury, mood swings, emptiness, and inappropriate strong anger.
Causes are not fully known, but environmental factors such as childhood abuse or neglect, genetics, and brain changes may be involved.

Risk factors include hereditary predisposition and stressful childhood experiences such as abuse, neglect, separation, caregiver substance misuse or mental health issues, hostile conflict, and unstable family relationships.

Complications may include job changes or losses, not finishing education, legal issues, conflict-filled relationships, marital stress or divorce, self-injury, hospital stays, abusive relationships, unplanned pregnancies, STIs, motor vehicle accidents, physical fights, attempted suicide or death by suicide, and other mental health conditions including depression, substance misuse, anxiety, eating disorders, bipolar disorder, PTSD, ADHD, and other personality disorders.

helpful: Access to mental health treatment, crisis planning, DBT-style emotion regulation and distress tolerance skills, trauma-informed care, clear boundaries, non-abandoning communication, safety planning, flexible care during crisis, and coordinated treatment for co-occurring conditions.
effect_if_absent: Without accommodations and treatment access, symptoms can be moralized as manipulation, attention-seeking, or instability instead of treated as serious mental health needs.
Lack of crisis response, relational consistency, and emotional-regulation support can worsen self-harm risk, relationship loss, job loss, and hospitalization.
Without accommodations and treatment access, symptoms can be moralized as manipulation, attention-seeking, or instability instead of treated as serious mental health needs.
Lack of crisis response, relational consistency, and emotional-regulation support can worsen self-harm risk, relationship loss, job loss, and hospitalization.

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for: MS causes breakdown of the protective covering of nerves and may cause numbness, weakness, trouble walking, vision changes, and other symptoms.
In MS, the immune system attacks the myelin sheath covering nerve fibers, interrupting communication between the brain and body and potentially causing permanent nerve damage.
Symptoms depend on the person, nervous-system damage location, and damage severity. Some people lose the ability to walk independently or move at all; others have long remission periods.
Types include relapsing-remitting MS, secondary-progressive MS, primary-progressive MS, clinically isolated syndrome, and radiologically isolated syndrome.

Common symptoms include numbness or tingling, electric shock sensations with neck movement, lack of coordination, trouble walking or inability to walk, weakness, partial or complete vision loss, double vision, blurry vision, vertigo, sexual/bowel/bladder dysfunction, fatigue, slurred speech, memory/thinking/understanding trouble, and mood changes.
Small body-temperature increases can temporarily worsen symptoms as pseudorelapses.
Risk factors include age, sex, family history, Epstein-Barr and other infections, race, climate, vitamin D, obesity, autoimmune disease, smoking, and gut microbiome differences.

Complications may include mood changes such as depression/anxiety/mood swings, muscle stiffness/spasms, rare seizures, severe weakness or paralysis, bladder/bowel/sexual dysfunction, and thinking/memory trouble.

helpful: Accessible entrances, elevators, wide routes, accessible restrooms, temperature control, rest breaks, seating, remote participation, flexible scheduling, mobility-device charging access, accessible transportation, emergency plans that include mobility devices, written instructions, cognitive supports, and medical care coordination.
effect_if_absent: Without physical access, a person with MS may be unable to enter a building, use a restroom, attend work or school, receive care, evacuate safely, or leave if symptoms worsen.
Without cognitive and fatigue accommodations, MS symptoms can be mistaken for disinterest, unreliability, or incompetence.
Without physical access, a person with MS may be unable to enter a building, use a restroom, attend work or school, receive care, evacuate safely, or leave if symptoms worsen.
Without cognitive and fatigue accommodations, MS symptoms can be mistaken for disinterest, unreliability, or incompetence.

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for: PMOS is the new name for the condition previously known as PCOS, affecting 1 in 8 or more than 170 million women worldwide.
PMOS is characterized by fluctuations in hormones with impacts on weight, metabolic and mental health, skin, and the reproductive system.
The previous name reduced a complex long-term hormonal/endocrine disorder to a misunderstanding about cysts and focus on ovaries, contributing to missed diagnoses and inadequate treatment.
Experts reported that there is no increase in abnormal cysts on the ovary in the condition, and diverse features were often unappreciated.

The name-change process involved more than 50 patient and professional organizations, 56 organizations in the article body, more than 22,000 survey responses, multiple workshops, lived-experience experts, and a 14-year collaboration.
The three-year transition period is supported by an international education and awareness campaign, with full implementation planned in the 2028 International Guideline update.
Name-change principles included patient benefit, scientific accuracy, ease of communication, avoidance of stigma, cultural appropriateness, and accompanying implementation.

helpful: Endocrine-informed healthcare, metabolic monitoring, mental health screening, stigma-aware communication, accurate naming, culturally sensitive education, flexible scheduling for appointments, and symptom-validating clinical care.
effect_if_absent: Without accurate recognition, PMOS may be dismissed as cosmetic, reproductive-only, weight-only, or cyst-only, leading to missed diagnosis, inadequate care, and worsened mental/emotional burden.
Without accommodations for long-term endocrine symptoms, a person may face workplace, school, relationship, and healthcare barriers while being blamed for symptoms outside their control.
Without accurate recognition, PMOS may be dismissed as cosmetic, reproductive-only, weight-only, or cyst-only, leading to missed diagnosis, inadequate care, and worsened mental/emotional burden.
Without accommodations for long-term endocrine symptoms, a person may face workplace, school, relationship, and healthcare barriers while being blamed for symptoms outside their control.

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for: Reactive attachment disorder in adults often occurs when RAD was not treated during childhood.
RAD is described as a serious disorder where a child is unable to form strong attachments with a primary caregiver.

Adult signs can include detachment, withdrawal, difficulty keeping relationships, lack of affectionate expression, control issues, anger outbursts, impulsiveness, distrustfulness, inability to fully grasp emotions, feelings of emptiness, and lack of belonging.
RAD can drastically impact meaningful connections and may result in mental health problems and drug abuse.
RAD is not autism; RAD is associated with neglect and abuse rather than the same roots as autism spectrum disorder.
Treatment is individualized and may include CBT and DBT; co-occurring substance use disorder should be addressed simultaneously.

helpful: Trauma-informed therapy, predictable boundaries, consent-based communication, non-shaming relational repair, DBT skills, CBT skills, co-occurring substance use treatment, and support groups or safe community connection.
effect_if_absent: Without relational accommodations, the person may be treated as cold, manipulative, angry, or uncaring rather than as someone with attachment injury.
Lack of trauma-informed support may deepen isolation, distrust, substance use, and inability to maintain relationships.
Without relational accommodations, the person may be treated as cold, manipulative, angry, or uncaring rather than as someone with attachment injury.
Lack of trauma-informed support may deepen isolation, distrust, substance use, and inability to maintain relationships.

source_page_references.why_used: mobility aid definitions, device types, selection considerations, advantages, limitations, and usage guidance

source_facts_preserved_in_structured_for.source_uses: Manual wheelchair is for locomotion indoors and outdoors for people with mobility disability, injury, or surgery recovery; wheelchairs can enable mobility, independence, and everyday activities despite reduced mobility.

limitations_and_risks: Self-propulsion can require shoulder, arm, hand, trunk, and stamina capacity; transport chairs can make the user dependent on another person for propulsion.
A building can be technically close by but unusable because of steps, steep ramps, heavy doors, narrow turns, inaccessible bathrooms, cluttered aisles, gravel, snow, or curb cuts that do not line up.
Standard taxis, rideshares, and private cars may not fit the chair or may require unsafe transfers; if the wheelchair does not fit, the person may be stranded.
If a wheelchair user can transfer but the chair is left behind, the person may lose independence, safe seating, pressure relief, posture support, or the ability to leave.

helpful: Accessible buildings, doors, routes, bathrooms, charging, repair options, transport compatibility, safe storage, and backup plans.
Respectful handling of mobility aids as essential access equipment, not optional luggage.
effect_if_absent: A mobility aid may fail to provide real access if the environment blocks entry, transport, seating, bathroom use, evacuation, or emergency response.
A mobility aid may fail to provide real access if the environment blocks entry, transport, seating, bathroom use, evacuation, or emergency response.
